mnemonics.title: CACHE MAPPING TECHNIQUES
mnemonics.content: DIRTY: Direct Mapping uses blocks that are In a Row, Restricts where data can go, Yields conflict misses, Takes minimal hardware.

mnemonics.title: CACHE REPLACEMENT POLICIES
mnemonics.content: LRU: Least Recently Used - REMEMBERS what you touched LAST; FIFO: First In First Out - Forgets the old, Keeps the new; RANDOM: Rolling the dice each time.

mnemonics.title: WRITE POLICIES
mnemonics.content: WRITE-BACK: Back away, wait until REPLACEMENT; WRITE-THROUGH: Through to memory, do it SIMULTANEOUSLY.

mnemonics.title: LOCALITY TYPES
mnemonics.content: TEMP: Temporal - Time-based repetition; SPACE: Spatial - Space (location) based nearby access.

acronyms.usage: Cache replacement policy that evicts the block not accessed for the longest time
Cache replacement policy that evicts the oldest block regardless of access pattern
Cache coherence protocol states for multiprocessor systems
Allows devices to access memory without CPU intervention, related to cache considerations
A cache for virtual-to-physical address translations
Cache addressing scheme using virtual addresses for both index and tag

visualTips.visualization: Draw cache as a MATRIX: Rows are cache lines/blocks, columns contain Tag, Index, and Offset fields. Think of each row as a shelf holding a book (data block) with a label (tag).
Imagine a PARKING LOT where each car (memory block) has a FIXED parking space (cache line) based on its number. Multiple cars may fight for the same spot, causing conflicts.
Think of a FREE-SEATING classroom where ANY student (memory block) can sit in ANY seat (cache line). The teacher (comparator) checks all seats to find the right student.
A hybrid of parking and free seating: Rows (sets) have a few seats (ways). Each car can only park in one row but can choose any seat in that row.
Use a LIBRARY analogy: Cache is your DESK (fast, small), main memory is the STACK (slow, large). If book is on desk = HIT; need to walk to stack = MISS.
WRITE-THROUGH: Copy to both notebook and main file cabinet immediately. WRITE-BACK: Only update notebook, mark with asterisk (*dirty*), update file cabinet when notebook page is replaced.

analogies.analogy: Cache is like a teacher's DESK with frequently used reference materials. Main memory is the library shelves. The teacher keeps useful books at desk for quick access rather than walking to library every time.
Like your PHONE charger cable - you use it repeatedly throughout the day and keep it within arm's reach because you'll need it again soon.
When reading a newspaper, once you finish one article, you often glance at NEARBY articles because they're likely to be relevant - similar to how cache fetches adjacent memory locations.
It's like ordering food: If it's in your FRIDGE (cache hit), you eat in seconds. If not (cache miss), you must drive to restaurant (main memory), wait for preparation - takes much longer.
Think of your work desk (L1), office filing cabinet (L2), and building storage (L3). Smaller = faster access but less capacity. You check desk first, then cabinet, then storage.
In a shared office with multiple WHITEBOARDS, if one person writes on their board, others must erase their outdated copies to stay synchronized - similar to cache coherence in multiprocessors.
